content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 31 (NUM_031)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 31:2 -- "Avenge the vengeance" vs. "Take vengeance" vs. "Exact vengeance"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Avenge the vengeance of the sons of Israel from the Midianites" |
| LOGOS | "Take vengeance for the sons of Israel against the Midianites" |
| PNEUMA | "Exact vengeance for the sons of Israel against the Midianites" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Avenge the vengeance of the sons of Israel against the Midianites" -- the Hebrew neqom niqmat is a cognate accusative (infinitive absolute + noun from the same root), which is a binding convention to preserve with doubling. EMET's "avenge the vengeance" faithfully reproduces the intensifying Hebrew construction. LOGOS and PNEUMA collapse the cognate pair into a single verb phrase ("take vengeance" / "exact vengeance"), losing the emphatic doubling. The Vaticanus preserves the cognate structure with ekdikeson ten ekdikesin. The preposition is adjusted from EMET's "from" (min) to "against" as the sense is hostile action directed at Midian. EMET's "from" is hyper-literal for the min of disadvantage.

---

### 31:2 -- "you shall be gathered" vs. "you will be gathered"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "afterward you shall be gathered to your people" |
| LOGOS | "afterward you shall be gathered to your people" |
| PNEUMA | "After that, you will be gathered to your people" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "afterward you shall be gathered to your people" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. PNEUMA shifts "afterward" to "After that" and uses "will be" rather than "shall be." The modal "shall" better conveys the solemn prophetic force of the Hebrew Niphal imperfect. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

---

### 31:3 -- "Arm from among you men for the army" vs. "Arm men from among you for war"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Arm from among you men for the army" |
| LOGOS | "Arm men from among you for war" |
| PNEUMA | "Arm men from among you for war" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Arm men from among you for war" -- LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on word order and "war" for tsava'. EMET's "army" for tsava' is defensible but "war" better captures the context of being sent into battle. The LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus word order is more natural English while preserving the Hebrew content.

---

### 31:3 -- "to give the vengeance of YHWH" vs. "to execute the vengeance of YHWH" vs. "to carry out YHWH's vengeance"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to give the vengeance of YHWH on Midian" |
| LOGOS | "to execute the vengeance of YHWH upon Midian" |
| PNEUMA | "to carry out YHWH's vengeance against Midian" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to execute the vengeance of YHWH upon Midian" -- the Hebrew latet niqmat-YHWH uses the infinitive of natan ("to give"), but "give vengeance" is unnatural in English. LOGOS's "execute" is a formal equivalent that conveys the judicial sense of the passage. PNEUMA's "carry out" is acceptable but less precise. EMET's hyper-literal "give" is awkward. The genitive "vengeance of YHWH" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "YHWH's vengeance" (PNEUMA) as it follows the Hebrew construct chain more closely.

---

### 31:4 -- "a thousand from a tribe" vs. "one thousand from each tribe"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "A thousand from a tribe, a thousand from a tribe" |
| LOGOS | "One thousand from each tribe, one thousand from each tribe" |
| PNEUMA | "One thousand from each tribe" (no repetition) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "One thousand from a tribe, one thousand from a tribe" -- the Hebrew repeats 'elef lamateh 'elef lamateh, and EMET and LOGOS both preserve the repetition. PNEUMA omits the second occurrence, losing the deliberate Hebrew emphasis on equal tribal contribution. The repetition is preserved. "From a tribe" follows EMET's more literal rendering of the Hebrew lamateh (lacking "each"), while "One thousand" follows the convention of writing numbers in English words.

---

### 31:5 -- "delivered" vs. "mustered"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "there were delivered from the thousands of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "there were mustered from the thousands of Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "there were mustered from the thousands of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "there were mustered" -- the Hebrew vayyimmasru is the Niphal of masar, which means "to be handed over, delivered, mustered." In a military context, "mustered" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the standard rendering and clearer than the overly literal "delivered" (EMET). LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:5 -- "armed for the army" vs. "armed for war" vs. "armed for battle"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "twelve thousand armed for the army" |
| LOGOS | "twelve thousand armed for war" |
| PNEUMA | "twelve thousand armed for battle" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "twelve thousand armed for war" -- three-way divergence. LOGOS's "war" for tsava' is consistent with the rendering adopted in verse 3. PNEUMA's "battle" (milchamah) is a different Hebrew word. EMET's "army" confuses the force with the activity. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:6 -- "the vessels of the holy [place]" vs. "the holy vessels" vs. "the holy vessels"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the vessels of the holy [place] and the trumpets of the blasting" |
| LOGOS | "the holy vessels and the trumpets for the alarm blast" |
| PNEUMA | "the holy vessels and the trumpets for sounding the alarm" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the holy vessels and the trumpets of the alarm blast" -- EMET's "vessels of the holy [place]" inserts a bracketed gloss; "the holy vessels" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is a cleaner rendering of kelei haqqodesh. For the trumpets, EMET's "blasting" is too bare; LOGOS's "alarm blast" captures teru'ah precisely; PNEUMA's "sounding the alarm" is paraphrastic. A combined reading is adopted.

---

### 31:6 -- "in his hand" -- included or restructured
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the trumpets of the blasting in his hand" |
| LOGOS | "the trumpets for the alarm blast in his hand" |
| PNEUMA | "who carried the holy vessels and the trumpets for sounding the alarm" (restructured, no "in his hand") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in his hand" -- retained per the Hebrew beyado. PNEUMA restructures the sentence to make Phinehas the subject of a relative clause ("who carried"), eliminating the literal "in his hand." The Hebrew text explicitly reads beyado, and the literal preservation is preferred.

---

### 31:8 -- "upon their slain" vs. "along with their slain" vs. "along with those already slain"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the kings of Midian they killed upon their slain" |
| LOGOS | "they killed the kings of Midian along with their slain" |
| PNEUMA | "they killed the kings of Midian along with those already slain" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "they killed the kings of Midian along with their slain" -- the Hebrew 'al-chaleleihem uses the preposition 'al, which EMET renders hyper-literally as "upon." In context it means "in addition to, along with" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). PNEUMA adds "already" which is interpretive. LOGOS's rendering is adopted.

---

### 31:9 -- "wealth" vs. "wealth" vs. restructured clause order
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all their cattle and all their livestock and all their wealth they plundered" |
| LOGOS | "all their cattle and all their livestock and all their wealth they plundered" |
| PNEUMA | "they plundered all their cattle, all their flocks, and all their wealth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** EMET and LOGOS agree on clause structure (objects before verb). PNEUMA moves the verb forward and changes "livestock" to "flocks." The Hebrew miqneh can mean "livestock" or specifically "flocks." The EMET/LOGOS word order preserves the Hebrew fronting of objects before the verb bazazu. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

---

### 31:10 -- "in their settlements" vs. "in their dwelling places" vs. "where they had settled"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all their cities in their settlements" |
| LOGOS | "all their cities in their dwelling places" |
| PNEUMA | "all their cities where they had settled" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all their cities in their dwelling places" -- the Hebrew bemoshevotam means "in their dwelling places." EMET's "settlements" and LOGOS's "dwelling places" are both acceptable nominal renderings; LOGOS is slightly more precise. PNEUMA converts the noun to a relative clause, adding interpretive expansion. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:11 -- "of man and of beast" vs. "both of man and of beast" vs. "both of people and of livestock"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "of man and of beast" |
| LOGOS | "both of man and of beast" |
| PNEUMA | "both of people and of livestock" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "both of man and of beast" -- LOGOS's "both...and" makes explicit the partitive sense of the Hebrew min-ha'adam umin-habbehemah. PNEUMA's "people" and "livestock" are dynamic equivalents; "man" and "beast" follow the Hebrew 'adam and behemah more closely. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:12 -- Clause order and "by the Jordan of Jericho" vs. "by the Jordan at Jericho" vs. "by the Jordan across from Jericho"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they brought to Moses...the captives...to the plains of Moab, which [are] by the Jordan of Jericho" |
| LOGOS | "And they brought the captives...to Moses...to the plains of Moab which are by the Jordan at Jericho" |
| PNEUMA | "And they brought the captives...to Moses...at the camp on the plains of Moab, which are by the Jordan across from Jericho" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to the plains of Moab which are by the Jordan at Jericho" -- the Hebrew 'al-yarden yerechoh is a construct chain. EMET's "Jordan of Jericho" is hyper-literal. LOGOS's "Jordan at Jericho" conveys the locative sense clearly. PNEUMA adds "across from" which is interpretive (the Hebrew does not say me'ever). LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:13 -- "princes of the congregation" vs. "leaders of the congregation"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all the princes of the congregation" |
| LOGOS | "all the leaders of the congregation" |
| PNEUMA | "all the leaders of the congregation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all the leaders of the congregation" -- the Hebrew nesi'ei ha'edah can be rendered "princes" or "leaders." LOGOS/PNEUMA's "leaders" is the more natural English rendering and avoids the anachronistic royal connotation of "princes" in a tribal context. LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:14 -- "who were coming" vs. "who came" vs. "who were returning"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who were coming from the service of the war" |
| LOGOS | "who came from the service of the war" |
| PNEUMA | "who were returning from the military campaign" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "who were coming from the service of the war" -- the Hebrew habba'im is a Qal active participle ("the ones coming"), which EMET renders with the progressive "were coming." LOGOS uses the simple past "came." PNEUMA adds "returning" (not in the Hebrew) and paraphrases "service of the war" as "military campaign." EMET's participial rendering is most faithful to the Hebrew form.

---

### 31:14 -- "service of the war" vs. "military campaign"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from the service of the war" |
| LOGOS | "from the service of the war" |
| PNEUMA | "from the military campaign" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "from the service of the war" -- EMET/LOGOS agree on the literal rendering of tseva' hamilchamah. PNEUMA's "military campaign" is a dynamic gloss. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

---

### 31:15 -- "every female" vs. "every woman" vs. "all the women"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Have you kept alive every female?" |
| LOGOS | "Have you kept alive every woman?" |
| PNEUMA | "You let all the women live?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Have you kept alive every woman?" -- the Hebrew neqevah means "female," but LOGOS's "woman" is contextually accurate since the concern is about adult women (cf. v. 17-18 which distinguishes women from girls). PNEUMA restructures the sentence as a statement of accusation rather than a question, altering the rhetorical force of the Hebrew interrogative ha-. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:16 -- "Behold, they -- they were" vs. "Behold, these are the ones who" vs. "Look -- these are the very ones who"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, they -- they were to the sons of Israel, by the word of Balaam, to give a treasonous act against YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "Behold, these are the ones who caused the sons of Israel, by the word of Balaam, to commit unfaithfulness against YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "Look -- these are the very ones who, on Balaam's advice, caused the sons of Israel to act unfaithfully against YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Behold, these are the ones who, by the word of Balaam, caused the sons of Israel to commit unfaithfulness against YHWH" -- EMET's rendering is hyper-literal and syntactically strained ("they were to...to give a treasonous act"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both produce clearer English. "Behold" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "Look" (PNEUMA) as a convention for hen/hinneh. LOGOS's "unfaithfulness" for ma'al is more precise than EMET's "treasonous act" or PNEUMA's "act unfaithfully." "By the word of Balaam" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "on Balaam's advice" (PNEUMA) as a literal rendering of bidvar Bil'am. LOGOS adopted with minor adjustment.

---

### 31:16 -- "the plague was among" vs. "the plague came upon" vs. "the plague struck"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and the plague was among the congregation of YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "so that the plague came upon the congregation of YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "so that the plague struck YHWH's congregation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "so that the plague came upon the congregation of YHWH" -- EMET's "was among" is overly static. The Hebrew vattehi hammaggefah uses the consecutive imperfect, indicating a result clause. LOGOS's "so that...came upon" captures this consequential force. PNEUMA's "struck" is vivid but adds agency not present in the Hebrew verb hayah. "The congregation of YHWH" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the construct chain better than "YHWH's congregation" (PNEUMA). LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:17 -- Clause structure of the killing command
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "kill every male among the little ones, and every woman who has known a man by lying with a male, kill" |
| LOGOS | "kill every male among the little ones, and kill every woman who has known a man by lying with a male" |
| PNEUMA | "kill every male among the little ones, and kill every woman who has known a man by lying with him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "kill every male among the little ones, and every woman who has known a man by lying with a male, kill" -- the Hebrew places the verb harogu ("kill") at the end of the second clause, creating an emphatic frame. EMET preserves this word order. LOGOS and PNEUMA normalize the English by fronting "kill" in both clauses. The Hebrew's deliberate end-position emphasis is preserved per EMET. PNEUMA also substitutes "with him" for "with a male" (mishkav zakhar), losing the technical legal term.

---

### 31:18 -- "the little ones among the women" vs. "the young girls" vs. "the young girls"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all the little ones among the women who have not known lying with a male" |
| LOGOS | "all the young girls who have not known lying with a male" |
| PNEUMA | "all the young girls who have not known a man by lying with him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all the young girls who have not known lying with a male" -- the Hebrew hattaf bannashim means literally "the little ones among the women." EMET preserves this precisely. LOGOS/PNEUMA render "young girls," which is a clearer English equivalent. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for clarity while retaining "lying with a male" (mishkav zakhar) as the technical term (not PNEUMA's "lying with him").

---

### 31:19 -- "you shall purify yourselves" vs. "purify yourselves" (imperative form)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall purify yourselves on the third day and on the seventh day -- you and your captives" |
| LOGOS | "purify yourselves on the third day and on the seventh day, you and your captives" |
| PNEUMA | "purify yourselves on the third day and on the seventh day -- both you and your captives" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "purify yourselves on the third day and on the seventh day, you and your captives" -- the Hebrew titchatt'u is a Hithpael imperfect, which can be rendered as an imperative or jussive. LOGOS's bare imperative is clearest. PNEUMA adds "both" which is not in the Hebrew. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:20 -- "vessel of skin" vs. "article of skin" vs. "article of leather"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "every vessel of skin and every work of goats' [hair] and every vessel of wood" |
| LOGOS | "every article of skin and every work of goats' hair and every article of wood" |
| PNEUMA | "every article of leather, everything made of goat hair, and every article of wood" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "every article of skin and every work of goats' hair and every article of wood" -- EMET uses "vessel" for keli, but "article" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more appropriate for the broad range of objects meant. PNEUMA's "leather" for 'or is interpretive; "skin" is the literal rendering. PNEUMA's "everything made of" paraphrases ma'aseh ("work of"). LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:21 -- "the instruction" vs. "the law"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the statute of the instruction which YHWH commanded Moses" |
| LOGOS | "the statute of the law which YHWH commanded Moses" |
| PNEUMA | "the statute of the law that YHWH commanded Moses" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the statute of the instruction which YHWH commanded Moses" -- the Hebrew chuqqat hattorah uses torah, whose primary meaning is "instruction, teaching" rather than the later juridical sense "law." EMET's "instruction" preserves the root meaning of the verb yarah ("to teach, direct"). LOGOS/PNEUMA use "law," which narrows the semantic range. EMET adopted for theological precision.

---

### 31:21 -- "who were coming to the war" vs. "who had gone to the battle" vs. "who had gone into battle"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the men of the army who were coming to the war" |
| LOGOS | "the men of war who had gone to the battle" |
| PNEUMA | "the men of war who had gone into battle" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the men of war who had gone to the battle" -- the Hebrew habba'im lamilchamah means "the ones coming to the battle." EMET renders "men of the army" for 'anshei hatsava', but LOGOS/PNEUMA's "men of war" is the standard rendering. LOGOS's past tense "had gone" reflects the narrative context (they had already returned). LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:22 -- "copper" vs. "bronze"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the copper, the iron, the tin, and the lead" |
| LOGOS | "the bronze, the iron, the tin, and the lead" |
| PNEUMA | "the bronze, the iron, the tin, and the lead" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the bronze" -- the Hebrew nechoshet can mean either "copper" or "bronze" (an alloy of copper and tin). Since tin is listed separately, "bronze" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the standard biblical rendering and avoids confusion. LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:23 -- "waters of impurity" vs. "water of impurity" vs. "water of purification"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "with the waters of impurity it shall be purified" |
| LOGOS | "it shall be purified with the water of impurity" |
| PNEUMA | "it must also be purified with the water of purification" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "it shall be purified with the water of impurity" -- the Hebrew mei niddah is literally "waters of impurity/separation." EMET's "waters" (plural) reflects the Hebrew plural construct, but English convention uses "water" (LOGOS). PNEUMA renders niddah as "purification," which describes the function rather than translating the word; niddah means "impurity, separation." LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:23 -- "every thing that comes into fire" vs. "everything that can withstand fire"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "every thing that comes into fire you shall pass through fire" |
| LOGOS | "everything that comes into fire you shall pass through fire" |
| PNEUMA | "everything that can withstand fire -- you shall pass through fire" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "everything that comes into fire you shall pass through fire" -- EMET/LOGOS agree on the literal rendering of kol-davar 'asher yavo' va'esh ("everything that comes into fire"). PNEUMA interprets "comes into fire" as "can withstand fire," adding explanatory gloss. The literal rendering is preserved. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

---

### 31:26 -- "Lift up the head" vs. "Take the sum" vs. "Take a count"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Lift up the head of the plunder of the captivity" |
| LOGOS | "Take the sum of the plunder of the captives" |
| PNEUMA | "Take a count of the plunder that was captured" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Lift up the head of the plunder of the captives" -- following the established convention from Numbers 1:2 (se'u et-rosh), the literal idiom is preserved. LOGOS formalizes to "take the sum" and PNEUMA dynamizes to "take a count," both losing the Hebrew metaphor. "Of the captives" (LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's abstract "of the captivity" (shevi can be either) and PNEUMA's paraphrase "that was captured."

---

### 31:26 -- "heads of the fathers" vs. "heads of the ancestral houses"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the heads of the fathers of the congregation" |
| LOGOS | "the heads of the fathers of the congregation" |
| PNEUMA | "the heads of the ancestral houses of the congregation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the heads of the fathers of the congregation" -- EMET/LOGOS agree on the literal rendering of rashei avot ha'edah. PNEUMA adds "houses" (not in this verse's Hebrew) and converts "fathers" to "ancestral." EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

---

### 31:27 -- "those grasping the war" vs. "those skilled in war" vs. "the warriors"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "between those grasping the war, who went out to the army" |
| LOGOS | "between those skilled in war who went out to battle" |
| PNEUMA | "between the warriors who went out to battle" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "between those grasping the war who went out to the battle" -- the Hebrew tofsei hamilchamah uses the Qal active participle of taphas ("to grasp, seize, handle"). EMET's "grasping" preserves the concrete verb. LOGOS interprets as "skilled in" and PNEUMA collapses to "warriors." The literal rendering is preferred; one who "grasps" war is one who takes hold of the instruments or conduct of battle.

---

### 31:28 -- "raise up a tribute" vs. "levy a tribute"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall raise up a tribute for YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "you shall levy a tribute for YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "levy a tribute for YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you shall levy a tribute for YHWH" -- the Hebrew vaharemota mekhes means "you shall raise/lift a levy." LOGOS/PNEUMA's "levy" is a more precise English term for a compulsory contribution. EMET's "raise up" is literal for the root rum but less idiomatic. LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:28 -- "one soul from five hundred" vs. "one soul out of five hundred" vs. "one life out of every five hundred"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "one soul from five hundred" |
| LOGOS | "one soul out of five hundred" |
| PNEUMA | "one life out of every five hundred" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "one soul out of five hundred" -- LOGOS's "out of" is clearer English for the partitive min. PNEUMA's "life" for nefesh is acceptable but "soul" is the standard rendering maintained throughout. PNEUMA adds "every" which is implicit but not in the Hebrew. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:28 -- "from the man" vs. "from the persons"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from the man and from the cattle" |
| LOGOS | "from the persons, and from the cattle" |
| PNEUMA | "from the people, from the cattle" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "from the persons" -- the Hebrew min-ha'adam is "from the man/humankind." LOGOS's "persons" fits the census context. EMET's singular "man" for the collective 'adam is awkward. PNEUMA's "people" is acceptable but less precise. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:30 -- "keepers of the charge" vs. "who keep the charge" vs. "who keep charge"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the Levites, keepers of the charge of the tabernacle of YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "the Levites who keep the charge of the tabernacle of YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "the Levites who keep charge of the tabernacle of YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Levites, keepers of the charge of the tabernacle of YHWH" -- the Hebrew shomerei mishmeret mishkan YHWH uses a participial construction. EMET's appositive "keepers of the charge" preserves the participial form. LOGOS converts to a relative clause. PNEUMA drops the article before "charge." EMET's form is most faithful to the Hebrew syntax.

---

### 31:32 -- Number format and "flock" vs. "sheep"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "flock, six hundred thousand and seventy thousand and five thousand" |
| LOGOS | "six hundred and seventy-five thousand sheep" |
| PNEUMA | "six hundred and seventy-five thousand sheep" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "six hundred and seventy-five thousand sheep" -- EMET preserves the Hebrew compound number format (hundreds-of-thousands + tens-of-thousands + thousands) and uses "flock" for tso'n. LOGOS/PNEUMA consolidate the number into standard English and render tso'n as "sheep." While tso'n means "flock/small livestock" (sheep and goats), "sheep" is the conventional English rendering in this context. The consolidated number format is clearer. LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:35 -- "the souls of man" vs. "the persons" vs. "persons"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and the souls of man, from the women who had not known lying with a male -- all the souls [were] thirty-two thousand" |
| LOGOS | "and the persons -- from the women who had not known lying with a male -- all the persons were thirty-two thousand" |
| PNEUMA | "and thirty-two thousand persons in all -- women who had not known a man by lying with him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and the persons -- from the women who had not known lying with a male -- all the persons were thirty-two thousand" -- EMET's "souls of man" is hyper-literal for nefesh 'adam. LOGOS's "persons" is clearer. PNEUMA restructures the verse entirely, placing the number first and converting the parenthetical qualifier. LOGOS preserves the Hebrew clause order most faithfully while being readable. PNEUMA again substitutes "lying with him" for "lying with a male" (mishkav zakhar). LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:36 -- Number format
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "three hundred thousand and thirty thousand and seven thousand and five hundred" |
| LOGOS | "three hundred and thirty-seven thousand five hundred" |
| PNEUMA | "three hundred and thirty-seven thousand five hundred" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "three hundred and thirty-seven thousand five hundred" -- EMET preserves the Hebrew compound number phrasing, but standard English number format (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted for clarity throughout the census tallies.

### 31:40 -- "thirty-two souls" vs. "thirty-two persons"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "thirty-two souls" |
| LOGOS | "thirty-two persons" |
| PNEUMA | "thirty-two persons" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "thirty-two persons" -- consistent with the rendering of nefesh as "persons" in the census context (vv. 28, 35). EMET's "souls" is literal but LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus provides consistency.

### 31:42 -- "which Moses divided from the men who warred" vs. "which Moses divided from the men who had gone to war"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "which Moses divided from the men who warred" |
| LOGOS | "which Moses divided from the men who had gone to war" |
| PNEUMA | "which Moses had divided from the men who had gone to war" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "which Moses divided from the men who had gone to war" -- LOGOS provides a clearer past-tense construction. PNEUMA adds "had divided" (pluperfect) which is interpretive but reasonable; the simple past is sufficient. EMET's "who warred" is terse. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:43 -- "the half of the congregation" vs. "the half belonging to the congregation" vs. "the congregation's half"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the half of the congregation was" |
| LOGOS | "the half belonging to the congregation was" |
| PNEUMA | "the congregation's half was" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the half of the congregation was" -- the Hebrew mechetzat ha'edah is a simple construct chain. EMET's rendering is the most literal. LOGOS adds "belonging to" for clarity. PNEUMA uses the possessive form. EMET's direct construct is preferred.

---

### 31:47 -- "the one portion from the fifty" vs. "one portion out of fifty" vs. "one out of every fifty"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the one portion from the fifty, from the man and from the beast" |
| LOGOS | "one portion out of fifty, from the persons and from the livestock" |
| PNEUMA | "one out of every fifty, both of persons and of livestock" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "one portion out of fifty, from the persons and from the livestock" -- LOGOS balances literalness with clarity. EMET's "from the man and from the beast" is too literal for the collective sense. PNEUMA adds "every" and "both of" not in the Hebrew. LOGOS adopted.

---

### 31:48 -- "drew near" vs. "approached"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "drew near to Moses" |
| LOGOS | "approached Moses" |
| PNEUMA | "approached Moses" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "approached Moses" -- the Hebrew vayyiqrevu is "they drew near." Both renderings are valid; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "approached" is clearer modern English. LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

### 31:49 -- "lifted up the head" vs. "taken the sum" vs. "taken a count"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Your servants have lifted up the head of the men of war" |
| LOGOS | "Your servants have taken the sum of the men of war" |
| PNEUMA | "Your servants have taken a count of the men of war" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Your servants have lifted up the head of the men of war" -- consistent with the established convention of preserving the se'u et-rosh / nasa' et-rosh idiom (cf. v. 26, Num 1:2). LOGOS and PNEUMA dynamize to "taken the sum" / "taken a count." The literal idiom is maintained.

---

### 31:49 -- "in our hand" vs. "under our hand" vs. "under our command"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who [are] in our hand" |
| LOGOS | "who were under our hand" |
| PNEUMA | "who are under our command" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "who are under our hand" -- the Hebrew beyadenu means "in our hand." LOGOS's "under our hand" captures the idiom of authority. PNEUMA's "under our command" interprets the metaphor. EMET's "in our hand" is the most literal but "under" better conveys the authority nuance. A combined reading: "under our hand."

---

### 31:49 -- "not a man from us is missing" vs. "not a man is missing from us" vs. "not one man is missing from among us"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "not a man from us is missing" |
| LOGOS | "not a man is missing from us" |
| PNEUMA | "not one man is missing from among us" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "not a man is missing from us" -- LOGOS's word order is the most natural English. PNEUMA adds "one" and "from among" which expand beyond the Hebrew. EMET's order is awkward. LOGOS adopted.

### 31:50 -- "each [what] he found" vs. "what each man found"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "each [what] he found -- vessels of gold, armlets and bracelets" |
| LOGOS | "what each man found: articles of gold -- armlets and bracelets" |
| PNEUMA | "what each man found: articles of gold, armlets, bracelets" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "what each man found: articles of gold -- armlets, and bracelets" -- LOGOS's construction is clearest. EMET's bracketed insertion is awkward. "Articles" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is better than "vessels" (EMET) for keli in the context of jewelry.

---

### 31:50 -- "ornaments" vs. "pendants"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "earrings, and ornaments" |
| LOGOS | "earrings, and pendants" |
| PNEUMA | "earrings, and pendants" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "earrings, and pendants" -- the Hebrew kumaz is a rare term (only here and Exodus 35:22) referring to a gold ornamental piece. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "pendants" is more specific than EMET's generic "ornaments." LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus adopted.

---

### 31:50 -- "for our souls" vs. "for ourselves"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to make atonement for our souls before YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "to make atonement for our souls before YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "to make atonement for ourselves before YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to make atonement for our souls before YHWH" -- the Hebrew 'al-nafshoteinu is "upon/for our souls." EMET/LOGOS preserve the literal nefesh. PNEUMA dynamizes to "ourselves." The Hebrew nefesh carries theological weight in atonement contexts. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

### 31:51 -- "all [were] vessels of workmanship" vs. "every crafted article" vs. "all of it crafted articles"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all [were] vessels of workmanship" |
| LOGOS | "every crafted article" |
| PNEUMA | "all of it crafted articles" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all were crafted articles" -- a synthesis. EMET's "vessels of workmanship" is overly literal for keli ma'aseh. LOGOS's "every crafted article" and PNEUMA's "all of it crafted articles" both convey the sense. The consensus uses "all were crafted articles" to preserve the predicate structure of the Hebrew kol keli ma'aseh.

### 31:52 -- "which they contributed" vs. "which they set apart"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the contribution which they contributed to YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "the contribution which they contributed to YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "the contribution that they set apart for YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the contribution which they contributed to YHWH" -- EMET/LOGOS agree, preserving the cognate construction of terumah...herimu (contribution...contributed). PNEUMA's "set apart" loses this Hebrew wordplay. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.

### 31:53 -- Parenthetical treatment and tense
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "The men of the army -- they plundered, each for himself." |
| LOGOS | "The men of war had plundered, each man for himself." |
| PNEUMA | "(The men of war had each plundered for himself.)" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The men of war had plundered, each man for himself." -- LOGOS's pluperfect tense ("had plundered") correctly indicates this was prior action. EMET's simple past is less precise. PNEUMA adds parentheses not in the Hebrew, though the verse does function as an explanatory aside. "Men of war" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over EMET's "men of the army." LOGOS adopted.

### 31:54 -- "took" vs. "received"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold" |
| LOGOS | "Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold" |
| PNEUMA | "Moses and Eleazar the priest received the gold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "took the gold" -- the Hebrew vayyiqqach is "and he took" (root laqach). EMET/LOGOS render literally. PNEUMA's "received" is interpretive. EMET/LOGOS consensus adopted.
